summary: My name is Rothana Walbolt, and I was born in Battambang, Cambodia. I experienced the hardships of the Khmer Rouge regime as a child, being separated from my family and forced to work in rice fields. After living in the Khao-I-Dang refugee camp in Thailand, I immigrated to the United States in 1985, first settling in Portland, Oregon, before moving to Minnesota. Today, I serve as the Executive Director and Co-founder of the Minnesota Cambodian Communities Council, advocating for Cambodian arts, education, and leadership.

I was born in Battambang, Cambodia, a place that was deeply affected by the Khmer Rouge regime during my childhood. It was there that my early memories began before the war forced my family and me to flee. Battambang represents my roots and the beginning of my journey through hardship and survival.

Cambodia is my homeland, but it was a place of great suffering when I was growing up. The Khmer Rouge took control and tore families apart, including mine when I was just six years old. Despite the fear and starvation, Cambodia is where my story begins, and it shaped the resilience I carry with me today.

After escaping the dangers in Cambodia, my family and I lived in the Khao-I-Dang refugee camp in Thailand. I grew up there for several years, enduring the challenges of camp life but also finding hope through connections with family abroad. It was in Khao-I-Dang that we received letters from relatives in the United States, which eventually led to our immigration.

Thailand was a temporary refuge for my family and me after fleeing Cambodia. We lived in the Khao-I-Dang refugee camp there, which was a difficult but necessary place for survival. Thailand provided the space where we waited and hoped for a new life, eventually leading to our move to the United States.

The United States became my new home when my family and I immigrated in 1985. We first arrived in Portland, Oregon, and then moved to Minnesota to join other family members. The U.S. represented hope and opportunity, a place where we no longer had to fear starvation or war, and where I now work to support the Cambodian community.

When we first arrived in the United States in 1985, my family and I lived in Portland, Oregon for about five months. It was our initial landing spot before moving to Minnesota to be closer to other relatives. Portland was the first place where we began adjusting to life in America.

After living briefly in Portland, my family moved to Minnesota to join other relatives. Minnesota became our long-term home and the place where I became deeply involved in Cambodian community advocacy. Here, I co-founded the Minnesota Cambodian Communities Council to help preserve our culture and support our people.

I am the Executive Director and Co-founder of the Minnesota Cambodian Communities Council, or M3C. Our organization works to promote Cambodian arts, education, culture, employment, and leadership development in Minnesota. We also create spaces for elders and youth to connect, helping younger generations understand the sacrifices made by their parents and grandparents.

The Khmer Rouge was the regime that caused immense suffering in Cambodia during my childhood. At six years old, I was separated from my family and forced to work in rice fields under their control. Their brutal rule brought fear, starvation, and loss to my family and many others in Cambodia.

The first Fourth of July I experienced in the United States was in 1985, shortly after we arrived. The fireworks scared my mother because they reminded her of gunfire during the war, and she took us to the basement for safety. That day showed me how the trauma of war stayed with us even as we began a new life in America.
